workouts.name: Total-Body Bodyweight Circuit
workouts.notes: For anyone who wants to burn calories with zero equipment: run the five working moves as a circuit, 3 rounds, resting only where marked. Progress by trimming each rest 5 seconds per week until you're at 30s.

workouts.name: EMOM 18: Bodyweight Density
workouts.notes: 18-minute EMOM for people short on time: minute 1 burpees, minute 2 jump squats, minute 3 mountain climbers, repeated for 6 rounds — whatever is left of each minute is your rest. Progress by adding 1 burpee or 2 jump squats per round when you finish with 20+ seconds to spare.

workouts.name: Low-Impact Fat-Loss Circuit
workouts.notes: No jumping, no floor-slamming — built for beginners, bad knees, or downstairs neighbors. Move briskly between stations and keep rests honest at 45–60s; progress by adding 2 reps per exercise each week before cutting rest.

workouts.name: Pull-Up Progression Day
workouts.notes: For anyone stuck under 8 strict pull-ups — you need a bar, nothing else. Keep every pull-up set 1–2 reps shy of failure; when all 5 sets of 4 feel crisp, move to 5x5 before adding negatives. The negatives are the progression engine: 4–5 seconds down, every rep.

workouts.name: Push Progression: Dips, Diamonds & Pikes
workouts.notes: Pressing strength without a barbell: dips lead while you're fresh, then diamond and pike variations attack triceps and shoulders. Own 5x5 dips with a 2-second pause at the bottom before adding reps; if dips are too much yet, do them on a low bar with feet assisting.

workouts.name: Bodyweight Legs & Core Strength
workouts.notes: Single-leg work is how you build real leg strength without a bar — split squats do the heavy lifting, jump squats keep you explosive. Progress by slowing the split-squat descent to 3 seconds, then elevating the front foot; holds progress by 5 seconds per week.

workouts.name: Skill Strength: Handstands & Holds
workouts.notes: For calisthenics athletes chasing skills, not just reps: wall handstands and hollow holds build the positions, pikes and pull-ups build the pressing and pulling under them. Accumulate the handstand time in as few sets as possible; when 5x30s is easy, take one foot off the wall.

workouts.name: Push Hypertrophy — No Weights Needed
workouts.notes: Chest, shoulders and triceps volume for leaning out — every push-up rep on a 3-second descent, no bouncing. Take each set within 2 reps of failure; when you clear the top of every rep range, elevate your feet another notch rather than adding endless reps.

workouts.name: Pull Hypertrophy — Tempo Bar Work
workouts.notes: Back and biceps volume on a bar and a low rail: chin-ups while fresh, rows for the bulk of the work, every negative controlled for 2–3 seconds. If chin-up sets collapse below 6 reps, swap the last sets for extra inverted rows instead of grinding singles.

workouts.name: Leg Hypertrophy Burnout
workouts.notes: High-rep leg volume that keeps muscle while you drop fat — squats and lunges done slow and deep, wall-sit to finish when your quads are already cooked. Progress by adding a 2-second pause at the bottom of every squat rep before you ever add reps.

workouts.name: Mobility Reset + Easy Holds
workouts.notes: For the day after a hard session: slow stretches to reopen hips and spine, then two easy holds to keep the core awake — nothing here should feel harder than a 4 out of 10. Breathe long and slow through every position; if anything pinches, back the range off.

newExercises.name: Scapular Pull-Up
newExercises.movementPattern: Scapular depression and retraction from a dead hang
instructions: Hang from the bar with straight arms, hands just outside shoulder width.
Without bending your elbows, pull your shoulder blades down and back so your chest lifts an inch or two.
Pause for one second at the top of the shrug-down position.
Slowly release back to a full dead hang and repeat.
newExercises.formCue: Arms straight — shoulders away from your ears.
newExercises.commonMistakes: Bending the elbows and turning it into a mini pull-up, or rushing the reps so the shoulder blades never actually move.
newExercises.beginnerModification: Keep your feet on a box and press lightly through them to take some bodyweight off the hang.
newExercises.whyThisMatters: It teaches the shoulder-blade motion that starts every strong pull-up and bulletproofs the shoulders for hanging work.

formGuide.description: Hang from a bar with completely straight arms. Pull your shoulder blades down toward your back pockets so your body rises slightly, pause, then lower back into a relaxed hang. The elbows never bend — all the movement comes from the shoulder blades.
formGuide.diagramPrompt: Side-view illustration of an athlete hanging from a pull-up bar with straight arms, shown in two positions: relaxed dead hang and shoulder blades depressed with chest slightly lifted. Highlight the shoulder blades with directional arrows pointing down and back; keep elbows visibly locked in both frames.

newExercises.name: Negative Pull-Up
newExercises.equipment: Pull-up bar, box or bench to jump from
instructions: Stand on a box so your chin starts above the bar and grip just outside shoulder width.
Step or jump off the box and hold the top position for one second.
Lower yourself as slowly as you can — aim for a 4 to 5 second descent all the way to straight arms.
Step back onto the box and reset for the next rep.
newExercises.formCue: Fight every inch on the way down.
newExercises.commonMistakes: Dropping fast through the middle of the range, and skipping the full dead-hang finish at the bottom.
newExercises.beginnerModification: Shorten the range: lower only from chin-over-bar to elbows at 90 degrees, then step back up.
newExercises.whyThisMatters: Slow negatives build the exact strength and control that turn zero pull-ups into your first strict rep.

formGuide.description: Start with your chin over the bar by stepping off a box, then lower yourself to straight arms as slowly as possible — four to five seconds per rep. The controlled descent trains the same muscles as a pull-up while you build the strength to do full reps.
formGuide.diagramPrompt: Side-view three-frame sequence of an athlete at a pull-up bar: chin over the bar at the top, halfway down with elbows at 90 degrees, and a full dead hang at the bottom. Add a downward arrow with a clock icon indicating a slow 4–5 second descent.

newExercises.name: Wall Handstand Hold
newExercises.musclesWorked: Shoulders, triceps, upper traps, core, wrists
instructions: Place your hands shoulder-width on the floor about 15–25 cm from the wall, fingers spread.
Kick up one leg at a time until your heels rest lightly on the wall.
Push tall through your shoulders, squeeze your glutes and ribs down, and look at a spot between your hands.
Hold with steady breathing, then lower one leg at a time under control.
newExercises.formCue: Push the floor away — get tall through your shoulders.
newExercises.commonMistakes: A banana-back arch from flared ribs, and collapsing through the shoulders instead of actively pushing up.
newExercises.beginnerModification: Do a pike hold instead: feet on a box, hips stacked over shoulders, holding the same vertical pressing position at half the load.
newExercises.whyThisMatters: It builds the overhead strength, balance and body tension that every advanced calisthenics pressing skill is built on.

formGuide.description: Kick up into a handstand with your heels resting on a wall, hands shoulder-width and about a hand-length from the base. Push tall through the shoulders, keep ribs tucked and glutes tight so the body forms one straight line, and breathe steadily for the hold.
formGuide.diagramPrompt: Side-view illustration of an athlete holding a handstand with heels against a wall: straight line from wrists through shoulders, hips and heels. Highlight the stacked alignment with a vertical guide line, an arrow at the shoulders pointing upward for the push-tall cue, and a small marker showing hands placed a hand-length from the wall.
